entries.name: 1. Notes
entries.text: A historical account written by ‘Abdu’l‑Bahá around 1 and first published anonymously in Persian in 1890. The English translation was prepared by Professor Edward G. Browne and first published by Cambridge University Press in 1891.
entries.label: TN 1

entries.name: 1. Notes
entries.text: Muḥammad Sháh died September 4, 1848; the English translation of A Traveler’s Narrative first appeared in 1891.
entries.label: TN 1

entries.name: 1. Notes
entries.text: A reference to Bahá’u’lláh, “Him Whom God shall make manifest,” whose precursor the Báb considered Himself to be.
entries.label: TN 1

entries.name: 1. Notes
entries.text: Atabát ‘Alíyat, literally Supreme Shrines, a term by which the Shí’ih Muslims referred to the cities of Kazímayn, Najaf, and Karbilá and generally applied to the region of eastern ‘Iráq, of which Baghdád was the center. When Bahá’u’lláh was released from prison and banished from Persia, He chose Baghdád for the place of His exile.
entries.label: TN 1

entries.name: 1. Notes
entries.text: 1852. Hín, according to the Abjad notation, equals 68. Cf. The Dawn-Breakers, Chapter I, note beginning “According to the Abjad notation…”: In 1 Bahá’u’lláh, chained in the Black Pit of Ṭihrán, received the first intimations of His Divine Mission, and that same year hinted of this in His odes.
entries.label: TN 1

entries.name: 1. Notes
entries.text: Throughout His Writings the “Oppressed One” refers to Bahá’u’lláh Himself.
entries.label: TN 1

entries.name: 1. Notes
entries.text: Mírzá Yaḥyá’s title was Subh-i-Azal, the Morning of Eternity. Bahá’u’lláh, in this connection, cites Amos 4:12–13, which says that God “maketh the morning darkness.” Cf. Bahá’u’lláh, Epistle to the Son of the Wolf, paragraph beginning “O Shaykh! Reflect upon these words…”. See Shoghi Effendi, God Passes By, Chapter VII, paragraph beginning “As the character of the professed adherents of the Báb declined…” for other titles of Mírzá Yaḥyá.
entries.label: TN 1
